返回本批次

EFF 在德克萨斯州东区专利案中挑战保密性

EFF Challenges Secrecy in Eastern District of Texas Patent Case

www.eff.org 作者 hn_acker 分数 56 评论 2 2026/04/28 00:14
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 纽约大学法学院技术法律与政策诊所的诊所学生 Emily Ko 和 Zoe Lee 是这篇文章的主要作者。法院不是商业纠纷的私人论坛。它们是公共机构，其记录属于公众。但法院常常忘记这一点，并允许大规模的过度密封，尤其是在专利案件中。 EFF 最近在德克萨斯州东区发现了另一起此类案件，该地区有关数十亿人每天使用的 Wi-Fi 技术的重要法庭文件完全被隐藏在公众视野之外。公众无法看到双方关于专利所有权、原告在法庭上的地位或与标准化技术相关的许可义务的论点。 EFF 寻求揭露 Wilus 案件中的密封信息 Wilus 标准与技术研究所诉惠普公司一案凸显了专利诉讼中反复出现的透明度问题。 Wilus 声称拥有与 Wi-Fi 6（嵌入日常设备中的技术）相关的标准必要专利 (SEP)。 Wilus 起诉三星和惠普侵犯专利权。惠普辩称，Wilus 未能按照公平、合理和非歧视 (FRAND) 条款提供许可，而这些条款是防止标准必要专利持有者利用其地位、阻止公平获取广泛使用的技术所必需的。在审查案卷时，EFF 发现许多文件在宽松的保护令下被不当密封，而没有适当动议密封所需的具体理由。由于假定公众可以查阅法庭文件，因此诉讼当事人必须提出动议以密封并证明令人信服的保密理由。这通常需要逐个文档、逐行进行论证。在德克萨斯州东区，该标准通常没有得到执行。相反，地方法官允许诉讼当事人在保护令中使用样板理由隐藏信息，而无需解释为什么应隐藏特定文件或文件中的特定部分。在Wilus 中，有两套文件引人注目。首先，三星提出驳回此案，认为 Wilus 可能没有有效获得这些专利，这引发了人们对他们是否有资格提起诉讼的怀疑。威鲁斯对该动议的反对意见是完全密封提交的，根本没有经过编辑的公开版本。该简报可能涉及支撑 Wilus 商业模式的专利转让协议——公众感兴趣的信息，特别是在涉及像 Wilus 这样的非执业实体 (NPE) 的案件中。其次，与惠普关于 FRAND 义务的补充简报相关的文件也被完全密封，没有向公众提供任何经过编辑的版本。 Wilus 是否受 FRAND 约束的影响远远超出了本案。受 FRAND 约束的公司必须遵守合理的许可条款，而不遵守的公司可能会收取高得多的许可费用。在这两种情况下，公众都被排除在与关键技术如何获得许可和控制直接相关的争论之外。 EFF 推动公众获取 EFF 向 Wilus 的律师提出了这些担忧，并敦促公众获取密封记录。 Wilus 最终同意提交几份文件的修订版本，这些文件现在的编号为 387、388 和 389。这个结果是进步，但不需要外部干预。法庭文件的公开版本应该是默认的，而不是在外部压力下协商的结果。即使现在，这些新提交的经过编辑的版本也掩盖了双方论点的很大一部分。公众仍然无法完全了解这个涉及日常使用的技术的案件是如何被提起诉讼的。为什么公共访问很重要 密封法庭记录的目的是很少见的。为了克服公开访问的推定，诉讼当事人必须提出令人信服的保密理由。这是因为开放法庭是美国民主的显着特征。公众、记者和政策制定者都有权观察诉讼过程并追究政府行为者和私人诉讼当事人的责任。有些文件确实包含商业秘密或商业敏感信息。但这并不意味着诉讼当事人应该能够隐藏信息而不解释原因。德克萨斯州东区允许诉讼当事人绕过解释原因的要求。 EFF 在试图干预德克萨斯州东区的另一起案件“Entropic 诉 Charter”时也遇到了同样的问题。同样的模式在Wilus案中再次出现：文件被批量扣留，而不是由特定推理支持的狭隘定制的修订。法院必须执行标准 法院（而不是第三方）负责保护公众的访问权。当然，这意味着强制执行“令人信服的理由”标准。应要求寻求密封敏感信息的各方证明每项拟议修订的合理性。德克萨斯州东区目前的做法还不够。经过它允许通过广泛的保护令进行广泛的、不受支持的封锁，它实际上默认将司法记录视为机密。案件繁重并不会改变规则。行政负担不能凌驾于宪法和普通法权利之上。司法记录假定是公开的。包括德克萨斯州东区在内的法院应该执行这一推定。其他联邦法院的做法是正确的 德克萨斯州东区是一个例外。在加利福尼亚州北区，法官通常会拒绝过于宽泛的封锁请求。正如查布里亚法官的《民事惯例令》所解释的：[M]动议密封。 。 。几乎总是没有优点。 。 。 。联邦法院的费用由公众支付，公众有权检查法庭记录，只有少数例外。备案方必须具体说明为什么其寻求密封的每份文件可以合理地被密封。 。 。对“竞争损害”的笼统而模糊的提及几乎总是不足以成为密封的理由。
  2. 这种方法反映了一条法则：密封必须经过严格定制并经过专门论证。法院透明度是根本 乍一看，专利诉讼中的保密似乎并不令人担忧。但这标志着透明度受到更广泛的侵蚀。德克萨斯州东区广泛使用广泛的保护令，如果法院不执行法律，这种做法就有蔓延的风险。这些做法使私人团体能够掩盖涉及塑造现代生活的技术的纠纷信息。这破坏了自由社会的一项核心原则：强大行为者行为的透明度。法院不是商业纠纷的私人论坛。它们是公共机构，其记录属于公众。只要这些做法继续下去，EFF 将继续倡导透明度并努力维护公众获取法庭记录的权利。

Original Summary

  1. Clinic students Emily Ko and Zoe Lee at the Technology Law and Policy Clinic at the NYU School of Law were the principal authors of this post. Courts are not private forums for business disputes. They are public institutions, and their records belong to the public. But too often, courts forget that and allow for massive over-sealing, especially in patent cases. EFF recently discovered another case of this in the Eastern District of Texas, where key court filings about Wi-Fi technology used by billions of people every day were hidden entirely from public view. The public could not see the parties’ arguments about patent ownership, the plaintiff’s standing in court, or licensing obligations tied to standardized technologies. EFF Seeks to Uncover Sealed Information in Wilus The case Wilus Institute of Standards and Technology Inc. v. HP Inc., highlights a recurring transparency problem in patent litigation. Wilus claims to own standard essential patents (SEPs) related to Wi-Fi 6 — technology embedded in everyday devices. Wilus sued Samsung and HP for patent infringement. HP argued that Wilus failed to offer licenses on Fair, Reasonable, and Non-Discriminatory (FRAND) terms, which are required to prevent SEP holders from exploiting their position, by blocking fair access to widely used technologies. In reviewing the docket, EFF found that many filings were improperly sealed under a lenient protective order without the required, specific justification needed in a proper motion to seal. Because there is a presumption of public access to court filings, litigants must file a motion to seal and demonstrate compelling reasons for secrecy. This typically requires a document-by-document and line-by-line justification. In the Eastern District of Texas, that standard is often not enforced. Instead, district judges allow litigants to hide information using boilerplate justification in a protective order without explaining why specific documents or specific parts in a document should be hidden. In Wilus, two sets of documents stood out. First, Samsung moved to dismiss the case, arguing Wilus may not have validly obtained the patents — raising doubts about whether they had standing to sue at all. Wilus’s opposition to that motion was filed completely under seal, with no redacted public version available at all. That briefing likely addresses the patent assignment agreements that underpin Wilus’s business model — information the public has an interest in, especially in cases involving non-practicing entities (NPEs) like Wilus. Second, filings related to HP’s supplemental briefing on FRAND obligations were also sealed in full, with no redacted versions available to the public. Whether Wilus is bound by FRAND has implications far beyond this case. Companies subject to FRAND must adhere to reasonable licensing terms, while those that are not can charge significantly higher licensing fees. In both instances, the public was shut out of arguments that bear directly on how essential technologies are licensed and controlled. EFF Pushes For Public Access EFF raised these concerns with Wilus’s counsel and pressed for public access to the sealed records. Wilus ultimately agreed to file redacted versions of several documents now available as Document Numbers 387, 388, and 389. That result is progress, but it shouldn’t require outside intervention. Public versions of court filings should be the default, not something negotiated after outside pressure. Even now, these newly filed redacted versions conceal significant portions of the parties’ arguments. The public still cannot fully see how this case about technologies that are used every day is being litigated. Why Public Access Matters Sealing court records is designed to be rare. To overcome the presumption of public access, litigants must show compelling reasons for secrecy. That’s because open courts are a distinguishing feature of American democracy. The public, journalists, and policymakers all have the right to observe proceedings and hold both government actors and private litigants accountable. Some filings do contain trade secrets or commercially sensitive information. But that doesn’t mean litigants should be able to hide information without explaining why. The Eastern District of Texas allows litigants to bypass the requirement to explain why. EFF confronted this very same issue in its attempt to intervene in another Eastern District of Texas case, Entropic v. Charter. The same pattern appeared again in Wilus: instead of narrowly tailored redactions supported by specific reasoning, filings were withheld wholesale. Courts Must Enforce the Standard Courts, not third parties, are responsible for protecting the public’s right of access. That means enforcing the “compelling reasons” standard, as a matter of course. Parties seeking to seal sensitive information should be required to justify each proposed redaction. The Eastern District of Texas’ current approach falls short. By allowing broad, unsupported sealing through expansive protective orders, it effectively treats judicial records as confidential by default. Heavy caseloads don’t change the rule. Administrative burden cannot override constitutional and common law rights. Judicial records are presumptively public. Courts, including the Eastern District of Texas, should enforce that presumption. Other Federal Courts Get It Right The Eastern District of Texas is an outlier. In the Northern District of California, judges routinely reject overbroad sealing requests. As Judge Chhabria’s Civil Standing Order explains: [M]otions to seal . . . are almost always without merit. . . . Federal courts are paid for by the public, and the public has the right to inspect court records, subject only to narrow exceptions. The filing party must make a specific showing explaining why each document that it seeks to seal may justifiably be sealed . . . Generic and vague references to “competitive harm” are almost always insufficient justification for sealing.
  2. This approach reflects the law: sealing must be narrowly tailored and specifically justified. Court Transparency is Fundamental At first glance, secrecy in patent litigation may not seem alarming. But it signals a broader erosion of transparency. The widespread use of expansive protective orders in the Eastern District of Texas is a practice that risks spreading if courts do not enforce the law. These practices allow private parties to obscure information about disputes involving technologies that shape modern life. That undermines a core principle of a free society: transparency regarding the actions of powerful actors. Courts are not private forums for business disputes. They are public institutions, and their records belong to the public. So long as these practices continue, EFF will keep advocating for transparency and working to vindicate the public’s right to access court records.

评论（默认中文）

Terr_ 2026/04/28 01:31
其他背景：德克萨斯州东部法院是“论坛购物”的目标，有某些问题的人（例如专利流氓）专门在那里提起诉讼，以联系有同情心的法官。
Additional context: The Eastern Texas courts are a target for "forum shopping", where people with certain issues (e.g. patent trolls) file cases there specifically to reach a sympathetic judge.
在 HN 中查看
silexia 2026/04/28 12:03
父母是一场噩梦，德克萨斯州东区法院是巨魔天堂。我很高兴看到 EFF 回归最初的使命。
Parents are a nightmare and the Eastern District court of Texas is troll heaven. I am glad to see EFF return to its original mission.
